did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a fully automated computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) system for skin lesion detection and recognition using deep learning. Specifically, it employs Mask R-CNN for lesion segmentation, DenseNet for feature extraction, and entropy-controlled least square SVM (LS-SVM) for feature selection and classification. The study validates this AI framework on dermatological datasets (ISBI2016, ISBI2017, HAM10000) to diagnose malignant melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel fully automated computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) system using Mask R-CNN and transfer learning for skin lesion detection and recognition. It details original experimental methods including data preprocessing, model training on ISBI2016, ISBI2017, and HAM10000 datasets, and reports specific quantitative results (e.g., accuracy of 96.3%, 94.8%, and 88.5%). The abstract and introduction explicitly state the proposal of a new framework and validation against state-of-the-art methods, indicating original research rather than a literature review.

title: Attributes based skin lesion detection and recognition: A mask RCNN and transfer learning-based deep learning framework
brief_summary: This study proposes a fully automated computer-aided diagnosis system for detecting and recognizing malignant melanoma using deep learning. The framework employs Mask R-CNN for lesion segmentation, DenseNet for feature extraction, and entropy-controlled least square SVM for feature selection and classification. The model was trained and validated on the ISBI2016, ISBI2017, and HAM10000 dermatological datasets. The system achieved classification accuracies of 96.3%, 94.8%, and 88.5% on these respective datasets. Additionally, the Mask R-CNN segmentation component demonstrated high performance with accuracies of 93.6% and 92.7% on the ISBI2016 and ISBI2017 datasets. The authors conclude that their proposed framework outperforms state-of-the-art methods in skin lesion detection and recognition.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on detecting and recognizing malignant melanoma. The abstract states the system is for 'diagnose malignant melanoma' and the introduction highlights it as one of the deadliest skin cancers. The study validates the framework on datasets (ISBI2016, ISBI2017, HAM10000) to achieve classification accuracy for this condition.

The introduction mentions that experts fail to discriminate 'malignant and benign nevi incidents', implying that benign nevi are part of the diagnostic challenge and likely present in the datasets (ISBI2016, ISBI2017, HAM10000) used for validation, even though the primary focus is on melanoma detection.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses Mask R-CNN for lesion segmentation. The abstract states: 'MASK-RCNN for the lesion segmentation' and 'MASK RCNN model is trained using the segmented RGB images'. Performance is reported as accuracy of 93.6% and 92.7% on ISBI2016 and ISBI2017 datasets.

all_task_types.target: Malignant melanoma vs benign nevi (diagnostic categories)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a CAD system for 'diagnose malignant melanoma' and 'discriminate malignant and benign nevi'. It uses DenseNet for feature extraction and entropy-controlled least square SVM for classification. The primary task is classification, achieving accuracies of 96.3%, 94.8%, and 88.5% on ISBI2016, ISBI2017, and HAM10000 datasets respectively.

datasets_used.name: ISBI2016
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset used for skin lesion segmentation and classification validation.

use.training: Used to generate ground truth segmented RGB images for training the MASK-RCNN model.
use.validation: Used to validate the classification accuracy (96.3%) and MASK-RCNN segmentation accuracy (93.6%).

datasets_used.name: ISBI2017
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset (also referred to as ISIC2017 in the text) used for skin lesion segmentation and classification validation.

use.training: Used to generate ground truth segmented RGB images for training the MASK-RCNN model.
use.validation: Used to validate the classification accuracy (94.8%) and MASK-RCNN segmentation accuracy (92.7%).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dermatological dataset (Human Against Machine with 10000 training images) used for classification validation.

use.validation: Used to validate the classification accuracy (88.5%).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

architecture_used.main_model: Mask R-CNN (for segmentation), DenseNet (for feature extraction), Entropy-controlled Least Square SVM (for feature selection and classification)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning-based (specifically DenseNet used for feature extraction, implying pretraining, though specific pretraining dataset not explicitly named in context)

architecture_details.feature_extraction_layers: Average pool and fully connected layers combined

training_details.training_data_for_segmentation: Segmented RGB images generated from ground truth images of ISBI2016 and ISIC2017 datasets
training_details.preprocessing: Decorrelation formulation technique applied to original dermoscopic images

classification_or_output_head.input: Feature vector from combined average pool and fully connected layers of DenseNet

preprocessing_techniques: Decorrelation formulation technique applied to original dermoscopic images
conclusions: The proposed fully automated computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) system, utilizing Mask R-CNN for segmentation, DenseNet for feature extraction, and entropy-controlled least square SVM for classification, effectively detects and recognizes malignant melanoma.
The framework achieved classification accuracies of 96.3%, 94.8%, and 88.5% on the ISBI2016, ISBI2017, and HAM10000 datasets, respectively.
The Mask R-CNN segmentation component demonstrated high performance with accuracies of 93.6% and 92.7% on the ISBI2016 and ISBI2017 datasets.
The proposed framework outperforms state-of-the-art methods in skin lesion detection and recognition, as validated by fair comparisons provided in the study.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on accuracy as the performance metric, lacking sensitivity, specificity, or AUC which are critical for evaluating diagnostic tests in imbalanced clinical datasets.

The paper does not report dataset size distributions (total, training, validation, testing splits) or per-class sample counts for ISBI2016, ISBI2017, or HAM10000, raising concerns about potential class imbalance and data leakage risks.

External validation is limited to three public datasets (ISBI2016, ISBI2017, HAM10000); no independent external cohort or clinical deployment data is provided to assess generalizability across different dermoscopic devices or populations.

The segmentation model (Mask R-CNN) was trained on segmented RGB images generated from ground truth of ISBI2016 and ISIC2017, but the specific preprocessing pipeline for generating these ground truths is not detailed, potentially introducing bias.
The classification accuracy on HAM10000 (88.5%) is notably lower than on ISBI2016 (96.3%) and ISBI2017 (94.8%), suggesting potential dataset bias or domain shift issues that are not fully analyzed.

The paper claims 'fair comparison' with state-of-the-art methods but does not provide detailed statistical significance testing (p-values) or confidence intervals for the reported accuracy differences.

The preprocessing step uses a 'decorrelation formulation technique' without specifying how it handles varying image qualities or artifacts common in real-world dermoscopy, limiting clinical relevance assessment.
